title: Gatorade Didn't Discover Thirst. It Replaced It.
description: In 1965, a University of Florida physiologist mixed salt, sugar, and water for a football team wilting in the Florida heat. Sixty years later, that formula — and the industry it spawned — had rewritten how the world thinks about thirst, funded the scientists who set the guidelines, and sold the concept of 'dehydration' as a medical emergency to people on 20-minute jogs. This piece traces how a legitimate sports physiology finding became a billion-dollar marketing operation, what the evidence actually says about when sports drinks help and when they don't, and why the advice to 'drink before you're thirsty' may have caused more harm than the dehydration it claimed to prevent.
heroImageAlt: Editorial illustration for Gatorade Didn't Discover Thirst. It Replaced It.
